steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Specific latent heat of fusion is the energy needed to…
options: Raise 1 kg of a substance by 1°C
Melt 1 kg of a substance at its melting point without changing temperature
Boil 1 kg of a substance from room temperature
Heat 1 kg of a substance from 0°C to 100°C
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Specific latent heat of fusion is the energy per kilogram to melt a substance (change state from solid to liquid) at its melting point — with no temperature change. It is not the energy to raise temperature (that is specific heat capacity).
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Specific latent heat of fusion = energy per kg to melt (or freeze) a substance at constant temperature. Specific latent heat of vaporisation = energy per kg to boil (or condense).
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.3.2 — Specific latent heat

steps.title: Specific Latent Heat
steps.bodyHTML: Specific latent heat (L) is the energy required to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without any change in temperature. The total energy for a mass m is given by E = mL. There are two types: latent heat of fusion (melting/freezing) and latent heat of vaporisation (boiling/condensing). The energy goes into breaking (or forming) the bonds between particles, not into changing kinetic energy.

relationships: Fusion: energy for melting or freezing (solid ↔ liquid)
Vaporisation: energy for boiling or condensing (liquid ↔ gas)
Temperature does NOT change during a state change
Bigger L → stronger bonds → more energy needed per kg

commonErrors: Confusing specific latent heat (E = mL) with specific heat capacity (E = mcΔθ)
Thinking the temperature changes during a change of state
Forgetting that condensing and freezing release latent heat (not absorb it)

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Drag the sliders to change the mass of water being boiled away. Watch how much energy is needed. Specific latent heat of vaporisation of water is fixed at 2 260 000 J/kg.

aqa.questionText: Calculate the energy needed to melt 0.4 kg of ice at 0°C. The specific latent heat of fusion of water is 334 000 J/kg.

steps.label: Identify the knowns
steps.content:
m = 0.4 kg
L = 334 000 J/kg
(no temperature change — melting occurs at 0°C)

steps.label: Substitute and calculate
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct substitution; 1 mark — correct answer with unit

aqa.questionText: How much energy is released when 2.0 kg of steam condenses to water at 100°C? The specific latent heat of vaporisation of water is 2 260 000 J/kg.
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark substitution · 1 mark correct answer + unit
aqa.commonMistake: Option D is the latent heat for 1 kg only — always multiply L by the mass, not just read off L.

equation.examTip: No Δθ here! Temperature does NOT change during a state change. Use E = mL for state changes, E = mcΔθ for temperature changes.

boardNotes.aqa: On AQA formula sheet. Know the difference between fusion (melting/freezing) and vaporisation (boiling/condensing).
